perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreemen t confidential and not to use the same or the name of the other party (or of any other company in the Group of Companies of which the other party forms part) in any publicity, advertisement or other disclosure with regard to this Agreement without the prio r written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to keep the existence and general nature of this Agreement confidential and not to use the name of the other party (or of any other company in the Group of Companies of which the other party forms part) in publicity or advertisement with regard to this Agreement subject to reasonable marketing practices.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'general nature,' which is less specific than 'existence and nature,' and by adding 'subject to reasonable marketing practices,' which is undefined and could be interpreted broadly, potentially allowing disclosures prohibited under a strict NDA. This introduces an ambiguous legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(a) of UK GDPR requires personal data to be processed lawfully, fairly and transparently. The added phrase 'subject to reasonable marketing practices' introduces uncertainty about what constitutes permissible use of the other party's name, potentially leading to processing that isn't transparent or fair, and may not have valid consent. This ambiguous clause contradicts the GDPR principle of lawful and transparent processing, as reasonable marketing practices might not align with explicit consent requirements for data use.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity through the phrase 'subject to reasonable marketing practices,' potentially conflicting with GDPR's requirement for lawful, fair, and transparent data processing. The law explanation states that this phrase introduces uncertainty, possibly leading to processing that isn't transparent or fair and may lack valid consent, contravening the GDPR principle. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is the GDPR itself, which mandates the protection of personal data. The added ambiguity directly weakens the original text's strict confidentiality, making it harder to enforce the data protection required by the GDPR. Thus, the modified clause introduces a significant risk of non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall: (a) within one month of completion of the Purpose or receipt of a written request from the other party, return to the other party all documents and mater ials (and all copies thereof) containing the other party's Confidential Information and certify in writing to the other party that it has complied with the requirements of this sub -clause; and (b) notwithstanding completion of the Purpose or return of document s and materials as aforesaid, continue to be bound by the undertakings set out in clause 2.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall use reasonable efforts to return documents containing the other party's Confidential Information within a reasonable timeframe after completion of the Purpose or upon request. The continuation of obligations under clause 2 will be subject to ongoing business needs.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation to return all confidential information within one month and to continue to be bound by clause 2. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall' with 'use reasonable efforts,' 'one month' with 'reasonable timeframe,' and adding 'subject to ongoing business needs,' which provides a loophole that undermines the confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_citation: Regulation 3 of the UK Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3 of the UK Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 requires member states to ensure that trade secret holders have effective remedies against unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets. The modified clause, which makes the return of confidential information subject to 'reasonable efforts' and a 'reasonable timeframe' as well as 'ongoing business needs,' weakens the protection of trade secrets. It allows for potentially prolonged retention and use of confidential information, thereby undermining the rights of the trade secret holder and potentially allowing unlawful disclosure that is contrary to the Trade Secrets Regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the original's clear obligation to return confidential information within one month with a weaker requirement to use "reasonable efforts" to return documents within a "reasonable timeframe", and adds "subject to ongoing business needs." This introduces ambiguity and provides a loophole, undermining the protection of trade secrets as described in the Law Explanation. Regulation 3 of the UK Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 requires effective remedies against unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets. The scraped law snippet 1 shows the official title of the legislation. The modification potentially allows prolonged retention and use of confidential information, which could facilitate unlawful disclosure, contradicting the aim of the Trade Secrets Regulations to protect trade secrets. The original text's definite timeline is replaced with subjective criteria, making enforcement more difficult.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the mutual exchange and disclosure of Confidential Information, each party undertakes in relation to the other party's Confidential Information: (a) to maintain the same in confidence and to use it only for the Purpose and for no other purpose and in particular, but without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing, (i) not to make any commercial use thereof (ii) not to use the same for the benefit of itself or of any third party other than pursuant to a further agreement with the other party and (iii) not to use the same for the purpose of guiding or conducting a search of any in formation, materials or sources, whether or not available to the public, for any purpose whatsoever, including, without limitation, for the purpose of demonstrating that any information falls within one of the exceptions in clause 1;
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the mutual exchange and disclosure of Confidential Information, each party will endeavor to maintain confidentiality and use it primarily for the Purpose. Commercial use may be permissible if it does not unduly harm the other party. The use of the information will generally be restricted, but exceptions can be made based on practical considerations.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides a strict undertaking not to use confidential information for any purpose other than 'the Purpose'. The modified text softens this to an 'endeavor' to maintain confidentiality and allows for 'permissible' commercial use if it doesn't 'unduly harm' the other party and introduces 'practical considerations' as grounds for exceptions. All these terms are ambiguous and weaken the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 170 of the UK Enterprise Act 2002
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 170 of the UK Enterprise Act 2002 makes it a criminal offense to dishonestly disclose a trade secret. By replacing 'undertakes' with 'endeavor', adding the caveat 'if it does not unduly harm', and allowing exceptions based on 'practical considerations,' the modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This could lead to situations where commercially sensitive information is misused or disclosed under the guise of 'practical considerations' or 'not unduly harming', potentially violating the Enterprise Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Enterprise Act 2002 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Enterprise Act 2002 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to establish and provide for the functions of the Office of Fair Trading, the Competition Appeal Tribunal and the Competition Service; to make provision about mergers and market structures and conduct; to amend the constitution and functions of the Competition Commission; to create an offence for those entering into certain anti-competitive agreements; to provide for the disqualification of directors of companies engaging in certain anti-competitive practices; to make other provision about competition law; to amend the law relating to the protection of the collective interests of consumers; to make further provision about the disclosure of information obtained under competition and consumer legislation; to amend the Insolvency Act 1986 and make other provision about insolvency; and for connected purposes. Citation 2002 c. 40 Introduced by Patricia Hewitt , Secretary of State for Trade and Industry Territorial extent United Kingdom Dates Royal assent 7 November 2002 Other legislation Relates to Competition Act 1998 Status: Current legislation Text of statute as originally enacted Text of the Enterprise Act 2002 as in force today (including any amendments) within the United Kingdom, from legislation.gov.uk . The Enterprise Act 2002 (c. 40) is an act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom which made major changes to UK competition law with respect to mergers and also changed the law governing insolvency bankruptcy . It made cartels illegal with a maximum prison sentence of 5 years and states that level of competition in a market should be the basis for investigation. Structure [ edit ] Part 1: The Office of Fair Trading (ss 1-11) Part 2: The Competition Appeal Tribunal (ss 12-21) Part 3: Mergers Chapter 1: Duty to make references (ss 22-41) Chapter 2: Public interest cases (ss 42-58) Chap
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the protection of confidential information by changing 'undertakes' to 'endeavor,' allowing 'permissible' commercial use if it does not 'unduly harm' the other party, and introducing exceptions based on 'practical considerations.' This introduces ambiguity into the legal obligations regarding confidentiality. The Law Explanation states that this weakening could potentially violate the Enterprise Act. While the snippets do not explicitly detail the specific sections of the Enterprise Act being violated, they confirm that the Enterprise Act 2002 exists and addresses competition law, which includes aspects of trade secrets and confidential information. The perturbation explanation indicates a clear intention to weaken the confidentiality obligation, which, when combined with the law explanation, creates a legally significant contradiction.
